title: practical english usage - Part 353
content:
So means ‘ that much ’  or 'to that extent'. It is used when we  are talking about a 1  meaning 584  so  (deg  ree   adverb):    so   tired,     so   fast not (     . . .-rheu-PH-leai^e-a/FThursday:) It's more expensive to travel on Friday, so I'll leave on Thursday.

to mean ‘ it follows from what 1 have said’ .

So (but not then) can also be used when the same speaker connects two ideas, ‘ I'll he needing the car.' ‘ Then/So I suppose I'll have to take a taxi.' ‘ It's more expensive to travel on Friday.' ‘ Then/So I'll leave on Thursday.' from what you have said'.

So and then can both be used in replies, to mean 'since that is so', 'it follows 583  so   (eonjunetion)ɪnd          then vocabulary' •  585  so and not with hope, believe, etc good weather.) not I hope that we'll have good weather. (     / hope so, that we'll have We   do not use so before a that clause.

’ Did you lose? ’ I'm afraid so.’ not ’ Do you think we'll be in time? I hope so. (     I hope.)’ not 'Is Alex here? 7 think so. (     .../ think that Alex is here.)’ be afraid, instead of repeating words in a thɑ  t-c lause.

We   often use so after believe, hope, expect, imagine, suppose, guess, reckon, think, 1 instead      of  that-clauses 585  so   and   not    with    hope,      believe,        etc I had never before met so gentle a person. (= ... such a gentle person.) (►    1 87).

There is another rather formal structure with so + adjective + a/an + noun 9 so  . .. a  ...

not                                                                    or (     Would you be so kind and. ..     Would you be so kind to ...) Would you be so kind as to tell me the time? (= ... kind enough to ...) This is formal and not very common.

There is a structure with so followed by adjective + as to + infinitive.

8  so   ...  as   to   ...

I'm glad you're feeling so much  better. (     ...so better.) But we  use so much,  not so, before comparatives.

The jewellery is so beautiful! (     ... so much  beautiful!) - I've never seen so much  beautiful jewellery.

Her luggage was so heavy that she couldn't carry it. (     Her luggage was so - She had so much  heavy luggage that she couldn't carry it.

We   use so, not so much,  before adjectives without nouns (►  584.5 above).

7 so  and   so  much I've read so much  and learnt so little.

There were so few interesting people there that we decided to go home.

I’ve bought so many new books; I don't know when I'll read them.

or without nouns.

We   can use so much  and so many (►  587), so few and so little with 6 so  much,      etc For such, ►    597.

in your so beautiful country.) not I enjoyed my stay in your country, which is so beautiful. (     I enjoyed my stay not I didn’t expect such terrible weather. (     .. .so terrible weather.) So is not used with adjective + noun.

Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 3 1 vocabulary •  586  so with say and tell to ride on it?

‘ Mummy,  I've broken my train? ‘ I told you so. You shouldn't have tried I told you so usually means 'ɪ warned you, but you wouldn’ t listen to me’ .

2  I told     you     so.

‘ Megan's crazy? ‘ Who said that? ‘ I did?

‘ Megan's crazy? ‘ Who says so?' *Dr Bannister?

want to identify the speaker, we  prefer that. Compare: for statements, about reasons why  we  should believe them. When  we  simply Note that so is used in this way mostly when we  are talking about the authority Taxes are going up. Joseph told me so.

‘ You've got to clean the car? ‘ Who says so?

that she's going to be the next president.) She's going to be the next president. Everybody says so. (= ... Everybody says a that- clause.

So can be used after say and tell instead of repeating information in 1  instead      of  that-clauses 586  so   withsay      and   te// ______       ___ ‘ The Professor's ill? ‘ So I understand?

‘ Emily's getting married? ‘ Yes, so I heard?

It's going to be a cold winter, or so the newspaper says.

the speaker learnt something.

believe and a number of other verbs. This structure is used to say how We   can use so at the beginning of a clause with say, hear, understand, tell, 3   so   at  the   beginning        of  a  clause 1 don't think so. (More common than I think not.) Think is more common in the second structure.
